title: Did Apple (Finally) Get AI Right At WWDC?, Anthropic’s Worry, Microsoft vs. OpenAI
summary: Alex Kantrowitz and Ranjan Roy discuss Apple's AI strategy at WWDC, Anthropic's claims about AI self-improvement, and competitive tensions between Microsoft and OpenAI. The conversation explores whether AI companies can balance safety with market pressures and public company obligations.
writeup: A Big Technology Podcast Friday roundup previews Apple's WWDC AI announcements based on Mark Gurman's reporting, with hosts Alex and Ranjan debating whether Apple's scaled-back, execution-focused strategy is a genuine course correction. The central argument is that Apple's operating system control gives it a structural advantage over Meta, OpenAI, and Google in the personal-agent race, even though the specific Siri features being announced (Project Campo, Gemini-powered, swipe-down interface) are technically unambitious. The episode also flags Anthropic's public concern about runaway AI improvement and the emerging head-on competition between Microsoft and OpenAI.
key_points: Apple's new Siri, codenamed Project Campo internally, is powered by Google's Gemini model under a billion-dollar agreement and hosted partly on Google servers, which may create privacy tensions given Apple's data-protection branding.

The core WWDC AI bet is an OS-level swipe-down interface that lets users launch apps, send messages, add calendar entries, search the web, and control third-party apps through a unified AI interface, replacing the old voice-only Siri with a do-it-all companion.

Apple's structural moat is operating system access: even if Meta's or OpenAI's agents are more capable, they cannot reach iOS system functions the way Apple's own Siri can, which Ranjan frames as the enduring value of inertia and installed-base control.

Apple's business performance through this pre-AI period is financially robust (over $140 billion in its fiscal Q1 and $111 billion in the most recent quarter, with the stock up 56% in a year), suggesting the company has breathing room to iterate on AI rather than bet the company on it.

The hosts characterize the 2024 Apple Intelligence rollout as visionary-but-broken (citing the Bella Ramsey ad as an example of overpromising), and read the 2025 WWDC approach as a deliberate reset toward features that can actually ship.

A model switcher is reportedly coming to the new Siri experience, allowing users to select which underlying AI model powers their queries, signaling Apple's strategy of being a distribution layer for multiple AI providers rather than a single-model shop.
